registry.description: Goodness is the quality of being genuinely, structurally beneficial — not just pleasing or approved but actually doing good in the deep sense of the word. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary runs from the aesthetic (pleasant, beautiful) through the moral (righteous, beneficial) to the relational (kind). God is described as good at the very beginning of creation: what he makes is good because it reflects his character. Human goodness is derivative: a participation in and reflection of the goodness that originates in God.

groups.context_description: Term declares God's inner being as good — the foundational doxological assertion that God's character, name, steadfast love, and Spirit are good, and that his goodness governs his relationship to all he has made
Term names human moral character and conduct as good — the inner quality of the person who walks uprightly, acts honestly, and whose character is recognised and assessed as good before God and others

Term names goodness as inner experiential good — what is genuinely good for the human person: proximity to God, worship, waiting, communal harmony, and the reorientation of the inner life toward what truly satisfies

Term names comparative wisdom good — the better-than sayings of wisdom literature where inner and relational qualities are ranked as the greater good above material wealth, social status, or external abundance
Term names God's good word and promise — the covenantal faithfulness through which God declares, fulfils, and sustains his promised good toward his people

Term names the moral assessment of conduct, ways, or deeds as not good — the prophetic and wisdom verdict that certain inner orientations and behavioural expressions are contrary to the good God requires

Term names God's evaluative pronouncement on his creation — the divine inner-being action of judging-and-declaring-good in Gen 1, where the Creator inspects what he has made and pronounces it good or very good

Term names what is good-in-the-eyes-of / pleasing to / preferred-by an actor — the volitional-preference idiom in which the term is the predicate of someone's will or evaluative agreement, naming what they choose, prefer, agree to, or judge fitting
Term names a state of inner well-being — the shalom-condition of being-well, prospering, glad-of-heart, or well-disposed — the inner-being state experienced or sought by the human person
Term names goodness as an inner-being disposition and Spirit-produced fruit — a quality that fills the person and is completed by God, expressed in righteous conduct and resolve
Term names kindness as God's inner disposition of generous goodwill toward humanity — the divine attribute that leads to repentance, governs judgment and mercy, and is displayed supremely in Christ
Term names kindness as a Spirit-produced inner quality of the believer — the disposition to act with goodness toward others, listed as fruit of the Spirit and garment of the renewed person

884.notes: "If it seems good to you to come" / "go wherever you think good and right" — Nebuzaradan to Jeremiah; volitional preference

884.verse_text: Jer 44:17 But we will do everything that we have vowed , make offerings to the queen of heaven and pour out drink offerings to her, as we did , both we and our fathers , our kings and our officials , in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem . For then we had plenty of food , and prospered , and saw no disaster .
884.notes: "We had plenty of food and prospered" — Jeremiah's opponents recalling their false prosperity in idolatry; fits 884-006 (prophetic verdict on conduct as not-good — here ironic, the people's self-justification is itself the prophetic indictment) — borderline; could also be 884-NEW-WB. Lean: 884-006 since this is reported speech in a prophetic indictment context.

Dan 1:4 youths without blemish , of good appearance and skillful in all wisdom , endowed with knowledge , understanding learning , and competent to stand in the king’s palace , and to teach them the literature and language of the Chaldeans .

open_flags.description: G5544 chrēstotēs (kindness) shows a tripartite inner-being engagement pattern across its 7-verse active NT corpus: (1) presence — kindness as Spirit-produced quality of the renewed person (Gal 5:22, Col 3:12, 2 Cor 6:6) and as God's relational disposition toward humanity (Rom 2:4, Rom 11:22, Eph 2:7); (2) absence — Rom 3:12 'no one does kindness, not even one' (universal-depravity quotation from Ps 14:3 LXX), where the term names the moral-spiritual quality whose absence characterises fallen humanity; (3) distortion — not strongly attested in this term but cf. parallel pattern on G2168. Worth examining in Session B whether this presence/absence pole is a structural feature of moral-quality vocabulary in registry 067 (goodness) more broadly. Verse Context retained Rom 3:12 in 886-002 with notes annotation per DF-001 Option C; group description was not amended.

Registry 197 (authority) is the third-highest co-occurrence partner with R67 at 17 shared verses — higher than joy, soul, or wisdom. The likely driver is Group 884-008 (volitional-preference idiom, 40 verses) where 'do what seems good to you' formula appears densely in royal and political narrative contexts. Session D should confirm whether the R67-R197 connection is formulaic (driven by 884-008 social context) rather than conceptual before treating it as a substantive inner-being connection.

Psa 34:8 ('taste and see that the Lord is good') is a shared anchor with R49 (discernment). The 'taste and see' metaphor names an inner perception act — an act of spiritual discernment. Session D should examine whether tasting divine goodness is analytically an act of inner discernment (R49) and whether the encounter with divine goodness cultivates or confirms discernment capacity in the inner being. Also shared with R78 (hope) — refuge-taking as hope's shelter posture.

Pro 16:32 ('slow to anger is better than the mighty; rules his spirit than takes a city') is a shared anchor with R4 (anger) and R199 (dominion/self-mastery). The verse uses the comparative wisdom framework (R67 Group 884-004) to assert that anger restraint and self-rule are of greater value than external power. Session D should examine whether this verse establishes a programme-level hierarchy of inner-being characteristics where self-regulation (slow to anger, ruling the spirit) is superior to external achievement — and whether this hierarchy is structural across wisdom literature or specific to this verse.

Jos 23:14 ('you know in your hearts and souls, all of you, that not one word has failed of all the good things the Lord your God promised') explicitly names heart and soul as the site of covenantal knowing. God's good word (R67 Group 884-005) is received and registered in the inner being. Session D should examine whether Jos 23:14 establishes a structural link between R67 (goodness) and R183 (heart) / R182 (soul) — specifically whether the reception of covenantal goodness is a heart-and-soul event that connects the goodness and anthropological registries.

Est 5:9 ('Haman went out that day joyful and glad of heart') is a shared anchor with R97 (joy). The tov-lev (glad of heart) compound explicitly locates the inner well-being state (R67 Group 884-009) in the heart. Session D should examine whether the inner well-being state of 884-009 (shalom-condition, glad of heart) constitutes the same inner-being characteristic as joy (R97) or a related but distinct phenomenon. The Est 5:9 instance is diagnostically significant — Haman's tov-lev is immediately destroyed by one sight, revealing the fragility of inner well-being not rooted in God.

[Q002][PARTIAL] Extended or withheld — goodness as character/virtue does not fit the grace/gift 'extended or withheld' frame cleanly. What the evidence addresses: conditions under which divine goodness is experienced (trust/refuge — Psa 34:8; continuing in kindness — Rom 11:22) and conditions under which human goodness fails (not-good verdict 884-006; universal absence Rom 3:12). Question review note: 'extended or withheld' frame fits grace-type words better; recommend revised form for character words.

Session D should confirm whether this co-occurrence is formulaic (driven by 884-008 social context) rather than conceptual before treating R67-R197 as a substantive inner-being connection. Source: OBS-067-OBS-020.

The verse is the anchor for Group 884-004 (comparative wisdom) and names anger restraint and self-rule as inner-being characteristics whose goodness (better-than value) exceeds external power. Session D should examine whether Pro 16:32 establishes a programme-wide hierarchy where self-regulation characteristics (anger restraint, self-rule) are structurally superior to external achievement — and whether this hierarchy is consistent across wisdom literature registries. The compound tov-lev (glad of heart) locates the inner well-being state of Group 884-009 in the heart — both a joy-state and a well-being state. Session D should examine whether 884-009's inner well-being characteristic is a sub-category of joy (R97) or a distinct experiential state that joy happens to name in this instance. open_flags.description: Rom 11:22 ('Note then the kindness and the severity of God: severity toward those who have fallen, but God's kindness to you, provided you continue in his kindness') places a conditionality on the experience of divine chrēstotēs: 'provided you continue in his kindness.' This raises a fundamental Session D question: are divine inner dispositions (chrēstotēs, severity, love, faithfulness) unconditional as attributes of God's character, or are they relational as dispositions that are genuinely contingent on the human party's response? This question exceeds the scope of any single registry's analysis and requires cross-registry synthesis in Session D, comparing how conditionality appears across goodness, grace, love, and faithfulness registries. Source: OBS-067-OBS-044.

H2898 (tuv — goodness) is classified as XREF in R67 but shows 0 active verses in its owner registry R103 (love). Is this a data gap — the term was extracted but no verses classified — or a deliberate decision? A term with 0 OWNER verses may need investigation before Session D uses it as a cross-registry link.

open_flags.session_raised: Session B Stage 2 (revision via obslog v3)
open_flags.description: H2898 (tuv — goodness, nominal form) is listed as XREF in R67 with R103 (love) as its OWNER registry, but H2898 has 0 active classified verses in R103. This raises a data quality question: was H2898 extracted but never classified in R103, or was it deliberately left with no classifications? A term with 0 OWNER verses cannot contribute to R103's analytical portrait. Session D should verify H2898's status in R103 before using it as a cross-registry connection. Evidence: XREF table showing OWNER verse count = 0 for H2898. Source: OBS-067-OBS-005.

Tri-registry boundary review: Does the programme split of the TOV/AGATH root family across R65 (generosity — active doing), R67 (goodness — quality/disposition), and R103 (love — relational-affective) produce sustainable analytical boundaries under the verse evidence? H2896A frequently spans all three within a single verse. The boundary is a programme-level analytical decision that Session D should test under cross-registry synthesis. Evidence: XREF structure (9 terms to R65 and R103); G0019 Gal 5:22 glossed as 'generosity' (R65 overlap); H2895/H2898 in R103.

Mic 6:8 ('He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the Lord require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?') is a cross-registry anchor shared with 6 registries: compassion (R23), condemnation (R24), humility (R80), kindness (R99), love (R103), will (R173). Session D should examine whether these 6 co-anchoring registries constitute a unified moral posture — justice + loving-kindness + humble walk as co-constitutive expressions of what is good — or whether they are better understood as distinct characteristics that happen to co-appear at this verse. This verse is the highest-density cross-registry synthesis node in R67.

Gal 5:22 (Spirit fruit list) is a cross-registry anchor shared with 5 registries: faith (R59), joy (R97), love (R103), patience (R116), peace (R117). This verse uniquely anchors both OWNER Greek terms of R67 simultaneously — G0019 agathowsunee (Group 885-001) and G5544 chreestotees (Group 886-002). Session D should examine whether the Gal 5:22 Spirit-fruit cluster constitutes a programme-defined cross-registry synthesis unit — 9 inner-being qualities co-produced by the Spirit as facets of a unified character — or whether each characteristic is independently Spirit-produced and separately analysable.

Eze 36:31 is a shared anchor between R67 (Group 884-006 — not-good moral verdict) and R1 (abomination). The verse places recognition of deeds as not-good alongside 'iniquities and abominations' — self-loathing for the latter follows from recognition of the former. Session D should examine: (a) whether the not-good and abomination registers share an inner-being mechanism (both produce self-directed moral revulsion); (b) whether Eze 36:31 establishes a causal chain from not-good recognition to self-loathing that connects R67 and R1; and (c) whether Rom 2:4 (divine chreestotees leads to repentance) extends this sequence to a goodness-abomination-repentance arc.

[Q001] Structural disposition — origin. Goodness originates in God. Psa 119:68 models the being-doing relationship: God is good (ontological) and does good (functional). Gen 1:31 establishes goodness as the ontological baseline — creation declared very good before any human moral life. Mic 6:8 positions goodness as divinely revealed. Human goodness is structurally derivative: responsive, enabled, and participatory. Anchor verses: Psa 119:68, Gen 1:31, Mic 6:8.

[Q003] Distinct modes of operation — 12 identified: (1) Doxological assertion (884-001); (2) Human moral character (884-002); (3) Experiential orientation/nearness (884-003); (4) Comparative wisdom evaluation (884-004); (5) Covenantal word/promise (884-005); (6) Not-good moral verdict (884-006); (7) Creative pronouncement (884-007); (8) Volitional preference (884-008); (9) Inner well-being/shalom-condition (884-009); (10) Spirit-produced virtue/agathosune (885-001); (11) Divine generous goodwill/chreestotees (886-001); (12) Spirit-produced kindness (886-002). DIM-67-001 resolved: these are genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena unified by theological origin in God.

[Q004] What goodness produces in the inner being: encounter with divine goodness produces trust/refuge (Psa 34:8), praise and thanksgiving (884-001), receptive desire for instruction (Psa 119:68). Experiential good of nearness produces stable inner orientation and testimony overflow (Psa 73:28). Reception of God's covenantal word produces inner knowing in heart and soul (Jos 23:14). Spirit-produced goodness fills the person and enables community (Rom 15:14). Recognition of not-goodness produces self-loathing and first movement toward moral transformation (Eze 36:31).

[Q006] What goodness reveals about God's disposition toward humanity: God's character is fundamentally good — his steadfast love, name, and Spirit are all described as good (884-001). His covenantal word does not fail (884-005, Jos 23:14). His creative pronouncement declares all of creation very good (884-007, Gen 1:31). His chreestotees is generous goodwill that leads to repentance (886-001, Rom 2:4) and is displayed supremely in Christ (Eph 2:7). It operates in a relational field with severity as its counterpart (Rom 11:22).

[Q014] Inner condition as ground of genuine goodness expression — three grounds converge: (1) proximity/orientation toward God (Psa 73:28 — 'it is good to be near God'); (2) fear of the Lord (Pro 15:16 — 'better a little with the fear of the Lord'); (3) Spirit-filling (Gal 5:22 — Spirit as enabling source). These three are not independent — they converge on a single inner posture: the person oriented toward God, filled by his Spirit, whose inner life has been reoriented toward the source of goodness.

[Q019] Shared etymological roots — extensive. TOV root family distributed across three registries: R67 (H2896A adjectival — quality); R65 (G0018 agathos and agatho- compounds — active doing/generosity); R103 (H2895 tov verbal — be pleasing; H2898 tuv — goodness nominal; relational-affective). Greek: G0019 agathosune shares root with G0018 and all agatho- compounds (R65). G5544 chreestotees shares root with G5541 (chreesteuomai — be kind), G5543 (chreestos — good/kind), G5542 (chreestologia — smooth talk, the distortion form).

[Q021] Structural opposites — three forms: (1) Direct negative — Group 884-006 (22 verses, not-good) names specific conduct contrary to what is good: rebellion, partiality, violence, false weights, self-direction. (2) Universal moral absence — Rom 3:12 ('no one does good, not even one') — the fallen condition is structurally characterised by complete absence of chreestotees: turned aside, become worthless, unable to do good. (3) Distortion — calling evil good (Mal 2:17); redefining goodness as material advantage (Jer 44:17); G5542 chreestologia (smooth talk) as etymological distortion form.

[Q028] Inner-being orientations that close off reception of goodness: (1) Self-direction — 'following their own devices' (Isa 65:2) — orientation away from God toward self-chosen ways; (2) Wilful refusal — 'we will not walk in it' (Jer 6:16) — the good way is offered and the will refuses; not ignorance but active resistance; (3) Moral self-justification — Jer 44:17 — redefining goodness as material advantage; (4) Unbelief/departure — Rom 11:22 — departing from faith closes off the ongoing experience of divine chreestotees.

[Q031] Mode of inner-being transformation — three distinct modes: (1) Reorientation — Psa 73:28: Asaph's inner life transformed not by changed circumstances but by shift of orientation; goodness as the transformed position not transformed situation; (2) Character transformation — Spirit fills the person with goodness (Gal 5:22, Rom 15:14) — the inner character itself changes; (3) Moral awareness transformation — Eze 36:31: the person who did not see deeds as not-good comes through remembering, recognising, and loathing to see clearly — moral blindness is the condition, recognition is the turn, loathing is the inner event marking the boundary.

[Q039] Source of capacity to extend goodness — transformation, not discipline. Spirit-produced: Gal 5:22 (fruit, not discipline); Col 3:12 (put on as response to prior transformation — 'God's chosen ones, holy and beloved'). Rom 3:12 confirms: natural human condition without transformation is characterised by complete absence of goodness — capacity must come from outside. However, Mic 6:8 presents genuine human action as required — the transformed person still has an active role in expression. Source is transformation; discipline may shape expression but does not generate capacity.

[Q040] Goodness reconstitutes rather than displaces human effort. 2Th 1:11: 'may God fulfil every resolve for good and every work of faith by his power' — the human resolve is real, not absorbed or overridden, but completed by God's power. Mic 6:8 requires active human doing. Spirit produces character (Gal 5:22) but Col 3:12 frames expression as active choice ('put on therefore'). Pro 16:32 names ruling one's spirit as a human achievement within a God-ordered value framework. Structure throughout: divine enabling → genuine human action → divine completion.

[Q042] Primary verse — Mic 6:8 as definitional anchor for human moral goodness: 'He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the Lord require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?' Three observations: (1) Goodness defined by tri-fold content — justice (outward conduct), loving-kindness (inner dispositional), humble walk with God (vertical orientation) — co-presented as constitutive not optional. (2) Frame is revelatory — 'He has told you' positions goodness as divinely disclosed, humanly received. (3) Requirement is comprehensive addressing both horizontal and vertical relations. Shared anchor with 6 other registries.

[Q049] Goodness and moral failure — one of the richest connections in the registry. Eze 36:31: recognition of deeds as not-good produces self-loathing — moral failure is catalyst for deepest moral awareness. Rom 3:12: universal absence of goodness diagnoses the fallen human condition. Group 884-006 (22 verses) dedicated to not-good verdict. Goodness and moral failure are structurally related: goodness understood partly through its absence in moral failure; and recognition of moral failure is first movement toward goodness. Shared anchor at Eze 36:31 with R1 (abomination) confirms connection.

[Q065] Performed vs genuine goodness — three instances: (1) Mal 2:17 — prophetic indictment of distorted inner judgment that calls evil good; the moral assessment faculty is inverted. (2) Jer 44:17 — people claim idolatrous prosperity was 'good' — self-justifying redefinition of goodness as material wellbeing. (3) G5542 chreestologia (smooth talk) — using kindness-language without kindness-character; etymological distortion form of chreestotees. The genuine version (Mic 6:8) requires justice, kindness, and humility; the performed/distorted version substitutes self-advantage.

[Q088] H2896A (tov) root meaning and conceptual origin — root TOV with 10 sub-senses spanning aesthetic pleasantness (1a), higher-nature pleasantness (1b), ethical rightness (1j), intellectual goodness (1h), gladness/well-being (1g). Conceptual origin is phenomenological before ethical: begins with concrete experience of what is agreeable and beneficial, rises to moral and covenantal categories. Hebrew goodness is not first an abstract ethical standard — it is first the quality of things that genuinely satisfy and benefit, recognisable in creation (Gen 1:31) and in proximity to God (Psa 73:28). The moral and covenantal registers are expansions of this experiential root.

[Q090] Distinction between goodness as inner disposition and as outward act — structurally embedded in XREF architecture. Inner disposition: G0019 (agathosune — goodness as virtue) and G5544 (chreestotees — kindness as inner character quality) are OWNER terms in R67. Outward act: G0015 (agathopoieo — to do good) and G0016 (agathopoiia — doing good) are XREF in R65 (generosity). Programme has structurally separated inner disposition (R67) from outward act (R65). H2896A serves both registers in Hebrew — the distinction exists in Greek more cleanly than in Hebrew.

[Q099] G0019 (agathosune) as NT coinage — almost certainly coined for the NT; not attested in classical Greek or LXX. The coinage reveals: the NT needed a term for goodness as a named inner-being virtue distinct from the adjective agathos that applies to virtually anything good. Coinage says: goodness is now a specific inner-being quality, not merely a predicate. Its entire 4-verse NT corpus (Rom 15:14, Gal 5:22, Eph 5:9, 2Th 1:11) is Pauline and pneumatological. The development from Hebrew tov's ten-sub-sense breadth to NT agathosune's precision is pneumatological concentration: goodness in its fullest expression named as a Spirit-enabled quality.

[Q122] Strongest co-occurrence connection — R103 (love) at 23 shared verses, highest co-occurrence count. Connection confirmed at root level: H2895 (tov verbal — be pleasing) and H2898 (tuv — goodness nominal) are owned by R103 as XREF terms, sharing TOV root family with H2896A. Mic 6:8 holds love and goodness together as definitional — 'to love kindness' is one of three constitutive expressions of what is good. The simultaneity of love and goodness in Mic 6:8 implies that at the deepest level of the good person's character, love and goodness are not separable activities but aspects of a unified inner disposition.

[Q127] Verses making explicit structural relationships — three primary: (1) Psa 34:8 — tasting divine goodness produces trust/refuge; structural relationship named: goodness-encounter → trust. (2) Rom 2:4 — 'God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance'; structural relationship named: divine chreestotees is the mechanism that produces repentance. (3) Pro 16:32 — 'slow to anger is better than the mighty; rules his spirit than takes a city'; structural relationship named: inner self-mastery is superior to external power.

[Q138] Dual anchor verses and their significance — Mic 6:8 is shared with 6 registries (compassion R23, condemnation R24, humility R80, kindness R99, love R103, will R173). Gal 5:22 is shared with 5 registries (faith R59, joy R97, love R103, patience R116, peace R117). Dual membership reveals: these are the programme's highest-density cross-registry connection points functioning as synthesis nodes. The dual membership of Mic 6:8 reveals that goodness, justice, kindness, love, humility, and will are not independent characteristics but co-constitutive elements of a single comprehensive inner-being orientation that Mic 6:8 defines.

[Q146] Goodness and repentance connection — addressed and substantive. Rom 2:4 (Group 886-001): 'God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance' — most direct goodness/repentance connection; divine chreestotees is the mechanism producing repentance. Eze 36:31: recognition of deeds as not-good and self-loathing is the inner movement preceding repentance — sequence implied: recognition → loathing → repentance. Connection is present and substantive. The goodness-abomination-repentance sequence (SP-067-006) is flagged for fuller Session D examination.

open_flags.session_raised: Session B Stage 2 (revision via obslog v3)
open_flags.description: The programme assigns G0018 (agathos — adjective, 90 OWNER verses in R65 generosity) and G0019 (agathōsunē — abstract noun, 4 OWNER verses in R67 goodness) to separate registries on the basis of the adjective/abstract-noun distinction (doing good vs being/virtue of goodness). The verse count differential (90 vs 4) is stark. Session D should test whether this boundary holds under the verse evidence — specifically whether the R65 agathos corpus includes verses that engage goodness as inner disposition (R67 territory) and vice versa. Evidence: XREF structure showing 6/9 XREF terms flowing to R65; G0019 Gal 5:22 glossed as 'generosity' (R65 semantic range). Source: OBS-067-OBS-004.

open_flags.session_raised: Session B Stage 2 (revision via obslog v3)
open_flags.description: Group 884-003 ('goodness as inner experiential good — proximity to God, worship, waiting, communal harmony, reorientation of inner life') is currently assigned Dimension 05 — Moral Character. However, the anchor verse Psa 73:28 ('for me it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord God my refuge') engages spiritual orientation and experiential proximity rather than moral character in the primary sense. The group description's language ('what genuinely satisfies', 'proximity to God', 'reorientation of inner life') points toward a spiritual-experiential dimension closer to Dependence/Creatureliness (08) or a spiritual orientation sub-category. Dimension review should reassess this group's assignment. Source: OBS-067-OBS-030.

open_flags.session_raised: Session B Stage 2 (revision via obslog v3)
open_flags.description: Group 884-005 ('God's good word and promise — covenantal faithfulness') is currently assigned Dimension 05 — Moral Character. However, the anchor verse Jos 23:14 ('you know in your hearts and souls that not one word has failed of all the good things the Lord your God promised') has God's covenantal faithfulness as the dominant subject, not human moral character. The group describes what God's good word does (it is kept, it does not fail) and how it is received in the human inner being (in hearts and souls). Dimension 11 — Divine-Human Correspondence or a covenantal-relational dimension may be more accurate. Dimension review should reassess. Source: OBS-067-OBS-034.

Does the vocabulary include a spatial or architectural term — a place or structure associated with the word?
What does the semantic range of the primary term reveal about the breadth of the word's operation?
Does any term carry a somatic or physiological dimension — naming an inner organ or physical site as the word's location?
What does the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective) reveal about how the word is conceptualised — as object, action, or quality?
Does the vocabulary include a supplication term — a term for the act of seeking the word from another?
What does the supplication term reveal about the inner posture required to seek the word?
Does the vocabulary include a term for the mechanism by which the word is administered or conveyed?
What does the atonement or propitiation vocabulary (if present) reveal about the structural conditions under which the word operates?
Does any term reveal a classical-to-NT directional reversal — a term whose direction of operation changes between its classical use and its NT use?
What does the vocabulary reveal about whether the word operates primarily within or across relational boundaries?
What does the vocabulary reveal about the word's relationship to covenant — is it bounded by covenant or can it extend beyond?
Does any term in the vocabulary name the word as a divine possession — something that belongs to God and is located with him?
What does the root architecture of the vocabulary reveal about the word's conceptual relationship to adjacent characteristics?
Does the vocabulary include compound terms — and if so, what do the compounds reveal?
What does the vocabulary reveal about the word's temporal character — is it punctiliar, durative, or both?
Does the vocabulary reveal whether the word operates as a judicial category, a relational one, or both?
What does the most intensive or plural form of the vocabulary term reveal?
Does the vocabulary include terms that name the word's expression in speech?
Does the vocabulary include terms whose etymology connects to the body — and if so, to which part?
What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about the word's complete semantic range — from its most intimate to its most structural expression?
Does the vocabulary include a term that names the word's operation at the intersection of the human and divine — a term that holds both simultaneously?

Section 5 — Connections and Research Poi.question_text: What is the strongest co-occurrence connection for this word — which adjacent characteristic appears most frequently in the same verses — and what does the co-occurrence pattern reveal?
Does any verse indicate that this word and its most closely connected adjacent characteristic operate simultaneously in the same inner-being moment — and what would that simultaneity imply?
What are the three highest vocabulary-sharing registries for this word — and what does the pattern of sharing reveal about the word's structural position in the programme?
Does the vocabulary overlap with any adjacent characteristic extend to root-level architecture — a shared root rather than only shared individual terms?
How many of this word's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another cluster or characteristic — and is there any cluster that shares all confirmed dimensions?
Does any single verse make an explicit structural relationship between this word and an adjacent characteristic — naming the connection rather than merely exemplifying it?
What is the co-occurrence count between this word and the characteristic with which it shares the most anchor verses — and what does that shared anchoring reveal?

Where this word and an adjacent characteristic share a dominant directionality (such as both being primarily divine-to-human), what does the shared directionality reveal about their structural relationship?
What is the relationship between this word and human will in the verse evidence — does the word displace, bypass, or reconstitute the will?
How does the study describe the relationship between this word and calling — is calling a consequence, an expression, or a distinct phenomenon?
What inner-being posture connects this word and listening — and is that posture described as a precondition or an expression of the word?
How does the study distinguish this word from faith — and what is their structural relationship?
What is the described sequence from the word to peace — and what comes between them?
What is the vocabulary overlap and co-occurrence count between this word and guilt?
What is the relationship between this word and mourning — does mourning precede, follow, or accompany the word?
Does the study present this word and hope as two separate phenomena or as aspects of a single inner-being posture?
Does any verse function as a primary anchor in both this word's study and another word's study — and what does that dual membership reveal about their relationship?
Does any verse anchor both this word and another word — and what shared inner-being posture does that dual anchoring reveal?
Does any verse present adjacent qualities as aspects of a unified inner-being posture — and if so, what is their common source?
What functional overlap does the verse evidence suggest between this word and wisdom?
Does the word operate only in relation to moral conditions (guilt) or also in relation to conditions of incapacity — and what is the evidence?
What is the co-occurrence count between this word and goodness — and in what literary context does the pairing appear?
What is the co-occurrence count between this word and anointing, and between this word and blessing?
Is the connection between this word and forgiveness established through contextual inference or through the lexical form of the term itself?
Does the verse evidence in this study point toward a connection with repentance — and is that connection sufficiently examined or deferred?
Does a recurring posture appear across the word-seeking scenes in the study — and is its relationship to an adjacent characteristic examined or deferred?

Section 5 — Generic (gap addition R067).question_text: Where a registry's word functions in more than one dimension (both as a character attribute and as a comparative/evaluative operator), does the co-occurrence and correlation data reveal different connection patterns for different modes of the word?

registry_specific.question_text: For registries where verse context groups carry affective inner-being states (gladness, well-being, shalom-condition) that do not fit Moral Character, Cognition, or Volition, what dimension should be assigned? The current 10-dimension vocabulary may require an Experiential/Affective category for these groups.

For verse context groups that name inner affective well-being states (glad of heart, shalom-condition, prospering inwardly) that are not primarily moral assessments, cognitive evaluations, or volitional acts, should the dimension be Emotion — Positive or Vitality / Existence? The two dimensions need clearer boundary criteria for these borderline cases.

For verse context groups where God's inner-being engagement is a creative-constitutive evaluative act (declaring creation good, establishing ontological goodness) rather than a relational disposition toward humanity, should Dimension 11 (Divine-Human Correspondence) apply, or should the dimension review introduce a distinct label for this ontological-creative register?
Does the comparative wisdom idiom (Group 884-004 — better-than sayings) operate as a distinct mode of goodness, or is it a subset of moral character?

What is the analytical relationship between agathōsunē (G0019 — goodness) and chrēstotēs (G5544 — kindness) as co-OWNER terms of this registry? Are they aspects of a single characteristic or genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena sharing a registry?
What does the Haman instance (Est 5:9 — tov-lev, glad of heart) reveal about the difference between genuine inner well-being and morally ungrounded inner pleasure?

What does the liturgical repetition of "the Lord is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (appearing as a refrain across Psalms 106, 107, 118, 136) reveal about goodness as a confessional and community-forming declaration?

Does the NT's coinage of agathōsunē as a new abstract noun signal a shift from tov's broad Hebrew range to a more specialised NT understanding of goodness as Spirit-produced virtue — and does this shift represent development or narrowing?

Does the programme's tri-registry distribution of the TOV root family (goodness as quality in R67, doing good in R65, relational pleasantness in R103) produce a coherent analytical triad for Session D, or does it create artificial boundaries that need to be addressed at synthesis?
